sentences.text: Salsitz.
9th.
September the 9th.
This was on a Friday.
And we looked out.
We knew the Germans are coming, because the day before, a motorcycle with a tank came in in our marketplace.
The day before, the Polish army was still in our town.
So the Polish army opened fire.
They killed the three soldiers, the Germans, and the tank turned back.
So we knew that they were coming.
Now, the next day, about two-- and meanwhile, a Polish unit from another town, from Mielec, came.
Kolbuszowa was already free.
No soldiers-- Polish soldiers-- because they left during the night, because they knew that they killed the Germans, so there would be reprisals.
The next day, in the morning, from the town Mielec, a new unit of cavalry came to Poland.
And they established themselves in the marketplace, in the houses, to make a line not to let the Germans in.
About two o'clock, the Germans came-- attacked, through the same road where the Jews came.
Not through the railroad or through roads, through their fields.
And they came in.
And in our house, we had a two-story house.
So I run on the attic.
And through the rear window I looked out, and I saw how the Germans approach our town, from the back, not through the roads.
And about two o'clock, they came in.
And we hid in our basement.
And we had, in this basement, maybe over 1,000 Jews.
Not Kolbuszowa Jews, Jews who were running away.
But when we knew that the Germans are coming, so they came in in the houses and they hid.
And I came down from the attic, and I saw the Germans are already closer.
And right away we could hear commands.
Forwards, this.
The Germans started to give out.
What they-- the first thing what they did, they put in four tanks in the marketplace, on four corners of the marketplace.
And the soldiers started to enter every single house.
Opened the door, and they started yell, raus raus, raus.
Out.
And naturally the Jews came out.
And they made a line for the Jews to go in a certain place.
In the--
No, just raus.
They didn't know who was there.
Raus, raus, raus.
And we walked, and they had a cordon on both sides with soldiers, and they took us outside of the-- out of the town.
My family, too.
And meanwhile-- and we left the houses like this.
The whole marketplace.
Meanwhile, they started to burn the houses.
Special with incinerator devices, they started to burn the houses, because they said that the civil population had the, the-- made the line, and they shot the German soldiers.
This day, in Kolbuszowa, 65 German soldiers were killed, because there was-- the battle was about two hours till they took it over. 65 German soldiers were killed. 125 Polish soldiers were killed.
And 30 Jewish soldiers were killed, which they were part of the Polish army.
Also were killed about 30 Jews from the refugees.
He didn't know where to hide and how to hide.
So during a cross fire they were killed.
No, no, no.
There was--
They were part of the regular--
They were part of the regular-- yes.
No, there were no separate units.
And they-- so they took us to a place not far from the town.
And they put us in an empty yard-- the thousands of Jews.
Everybody, their hands up.
And they burned-- they started to burn two houses next to us.
A colonel came, a German colonel.
He said, because you started to-- you fought against the Germans, so many Germans were killed, we are going to make-- to burn the houses all around, and you all will be burnt.
And, naturally, the women started to cry.
The religious Jews started to pray.
And everybody knew-- and the fire came closer and closer.
And we were staying there.
Then, about an hour later, the same officer came.
He said, well, we will not kill you, but you cannot go home.
And they put us in a field.
And we were in the field for a whole night, with the children.
Meanwhile, the town was burning.
They burned, in this town, half of the town.
And the next day we were in the-- in the field.
There are dramatic stories during the night, which I will not go into it.
You will see it in the book.
And they-- the next day, about 12 o'clock, they told us we can go home.
And everybody went home.
But all the houses were open, and everything was plundered.
Take out.
Our house.
Everybody's.
The Germans-- during the day, they came, and they took out everything from the houses.
And we came in in the houses.

And the next day, they started to take Jews to work, because there were hundreds of unused shells from the army, broken vehicles from the army, and also there were maybe 100 horses, dead horses, because this was a cavalry unit.
And when they fall, their horses were killed.
Now, a Jew was never used to handle dead horses.
So they started to grab religious Jews and they should make holes, ditches, throw the-- and bury the horses.
There were cases that there was-- I remember one man, and I remember even his name, that they-- he didn't work so good.
So when they made the hole, they put in the horse, and put the Jew under horse.
And they said to the people to cover him-- to-- to--
So those were-- every day started to be more and more vicious attacks on Jews.
Taken to work.
Nobody knew that today I will stay home.
And the Polish-- The Germans didn't know, in the beginning, where Jews lived.
Sure, there recognize the Jew with the beard, but there were Jews without beards.
So the Polish people, our people from our town, used to go around with the Germans.
They didn't speak German.
The Jews spoke German.
But one word they already knew-- Jude, Jude.
This word, they learned.
And they showed to the Germans, Jude, Jude, Jude.
They showed the Jewish ones.
And they came in, and they took the people to work.
And they worked every day.
Nobody was sure that he will be able to stay home.
Well-- and they didn't pay for the work.
I was taken to work also.
They came into our house, and they dragged me to work--
To clean up the mess The beginning was to clean-- to load the, the the equipment on weapons and to bury those-- and this took a long time.
This took maybe four weeks to clean up the mess.
Meanwhile, because we had a wholesale store, there was one Jewish fellow, and he started-- he was-- he became a friend from the German commandant.
In every town, you have somebody-- not a clean character.
And he started to make money with.
And he went to the Jews, and he said, well, give me this, give me this, give me this.
And before the war, he was living with everybody.
And, and, and then he came to my father, and he said, he needs for the commandant bohnen kaffee.
Bohnen-- coffee beans were very important for the Germans.
They didn't have it in Germany, and they liked coffee bean.
And my father had coffee beans in his business, so he came-- so my father gave him a kilo.
The next day he said, I want two kilos.
Then he said, I want four kilos, and so on and on.
So once I saw him, and I said, look, even a well, if you take out water, it will run dry.
I don't do it to my father, because I will take-- I will do-- and I threatened him.
Right away, he went to the German commandant, and he told them that I am very dangerous for the Polish army.
That before the war, I was in the Communist Party.
I didn't know about the Communist Party.
I was with peyes And Jews, usually in a small town.
We didn't have communists.
And especially, my father was a rich man.
So they said that they're going to arrest me.
And I found out about it, so I run away.
I run away to Russia.
And I went-- it was illegal on the border.
I was in Russia three months.
Where were you?
In Lemberg, Lwow.
In Russia.
And I started to do a little business-- black market.
But in Russia, I-- in, in Lemberg, I saw that I will accomplish my two dreams.
Why two dreams?
In this time, I was not religious anymore, so I enlisted in a gymnasium, in Lemberg.
This were [NON-ENGLISH] gymnasium.
And they accepted me.
So I think, well, I will go to the gymnasium, I will have this education, and maybe I'll become a doctor.
And the second thing, there was a Jewish theatre.
So I went to the Jewish theatre, and they took me in as being a member to start there.
So I knew that, here I'm going to the gymnasium, and I'm in the Jewish theatre.
My two dreams will come true.
I was in Germany about, about three-- in Russia about three months.
After three months, somebody came back and they told us that the mother of my girlfriend, which I love so much, is very sick, and she has only a few days to, to live.
And she-- this-- my girlfriend had her a father, a brother, and a sister.
And they said not to tell them, because if they will tell them that their mother is so gravely ill, they will come back.
They didn't want they should come back.
In Lemberg, also.
Yeah.
So, When I found out that she is dying, and she's alone with her, I said, I'm going home.
And I went home.
The same day I decided-- illegal on the border.
On the border, they caught me.
And then they-- it's a lot-- it's not for now-- that the Russians caught me.
And then later they kept us to take us to Lemberg, to jail.
But during the night, we were a few boys, so we disarmed the soldiers.
We took away their rifles.
With tied them down with the blankets.
We put them underneath the chairs.
And we came back.
I came home.
I am home.
I came home.
Three days later, she died.
The mother died.
And she was left alone.
She had her uncles.
And my father, I remember, the first thing, when I came home, and-- so he said to me, these words.
He said I'm very happy you came home.
But I think you made the biggest mistake in your life.
Of course, I came home, and I was young.
My father had a beard.
So I started to do business.
I started to run to other towns.
And I could go-- nobody-- in the beginning, we didn't have bands.
Later, we started to have bands.
Now, I could tell you days-- what happened every day.
First we couldn't walk on the street, on the sidewalk.
And then this, they they killed this-- it's a law-- every day was--
No, no.
In the beginning, in the beginning nobody wore a band.
But after a few months, there came out an order.
So everybody had to wear the band.
I had to wear the band too.
I did wear it.
But when I went out of town, I took off the band.
In this was during all the time when I used to smuggle in merchandise, when I used to do a lot of things.
I took off the band.
And I was never caught-- never caught as a Jew without a band.
Nobody recognized me [INAUDIBLE].
Later, when they established a Judenrat-- you know what a Judenrat is-- the Jewish community.
And the Judenrat organized a free kitchen, because the people started-- couldn't make business.
So, so they started to give food.
So they picked me out.
The Judenrat picked me out.
I should be the one to go out and buy food for the Judenrat, for the kitchen.
So even if we got, by the government, certain amount of flour and other things, but it wasn't enough.
So we had to buy it on the black market.
And because I didn't look Jewish, I went-- and also, I had a lot of peasants that they knew me from before the war, who used to buy by me.
So I used to go out three times a week, took off the band, and went out and brought in the food.
This went on--
For the soup kitchen, yes.
And also I brought for ours, for my family.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Yes-- we-- store.
I used-- when I used to bring this [INAUDIBLE], I used to buy some black-market things for the store and sell.
But we didn't have no merchandise in the store.
I continued to do this till the ghetto was established.
The ghetto was established in July '41.
They made a ghetto.
They took the Jews from our town.
And, and they, they had there a section which were the crooked houses where 70 families, Polish, lived.
And in this place, they put in 1,200 Jews.
It was very crowded--
The Polish families got out.
The Polish families got out.
They got the Jewish places.
Once we are in ghetto, it became very bad.
Because Jews couldn't go out to work, they didn't have where to live, they couldn't take in too much stuff in the ghetto, and it was very bad.
Then I went-- I, I still was the one who used to go out for the stuff.
So I used to bring in in the ghetto three times a week.
Again, the same story.
I went out, I took off the band.
And I came in, I put on the band.
And this went on.
And every day there were new, new, new, new orders.
Then I went to Rzeszow.
So there in Rzeszow was a dysentery epidemic, so I ate there something.
I came home, and I got sick with the dysentery.
They thought I'm going to die.
Finally, I, I, I managed to get-- to get out of it.
Two months later, I got a middle-ear infection.
And we didn't have no doctors, and I got the middle-ear infection for two months, and I almost died.
It was so painful.
Finally, the, the doctor said, if he wouldn't have an operation, he would die.
Because all the pus was around my brain.
So they got-- my brother got a permit to take me to Tarnow, to a larger city.
And I came there, and I got the operation.
I was there two or three months.
I came back.
I thought I would be deaf, but it healed, and everything was all right.
When I came back, in April 1942, the whole situation was changed.
The Germans, the Gestapo, came in every week, and they killed, random.
In the 20-- the 18th of April, they came with a list.
And they had 20 most important, prominent Jews [BACKGROUND NOISE] in the ghetto.
And they started to shoot the prominent Jews.
My father was on the list.
They came to my house, to our house, and they took out my father, and they shot him.
I saw it-- right in the-- well, in the yard.
And I--
I saw it.
I saw it.
And my-- they killed in this day 22 Jews.
They had on the list 20, but they killed two more.
And I saw them.
And they killed my father.
They shot him twice.
My father still lived, and he started to say Jewish prayers, Shema Yisrael and others.
And then, later, he started to insult the German Gestapo man.
And he started to yell nekome, nekome, nekome.
It means, revenge, revenge, revenge.
My father.
Take revenge.
He's so loud that, till today, 50 years later, I can  hear--
I can hear the voice.
And he-- it was not like a human voice.
It was a voice like it came from heaven.
And he-- he-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]
Yes.
This is what he said.
He also said-- he said-- take revenge, take revenge.
Then the German heard him screaming.
He came back, he shot him five more times, and he was dead.
He was dead.
So then, later, my brother and I-- my brother-in-law-- started to collect all the dead people, and we brought them to the cemetery-- Jewish cemetery.
We-- this was already in the evening.
We couldn't bury them, so what we made a shallow grave, and we let them [INAUDIBLE].
The next day, we came, we made a proper grave, and we buried everybody.
All the Jews--
All the Jews together.
But my father, we took out.
Because I put them in that-- on the wagon, and I put them so that their legs were sticking out.
And he-- my grandfather had there a grave.
And, next to his grave, my father saved this grave for himself.
And the reason he saved it-- because this was near the where the wagons could go in.
See, my father was a kohen.
A kohen couldn't-- I, as a kohen, cannot go in the cemetery.
So they prepared it near where there was place to go that the children should go.
So when my-- when the wagon went through-- so we pulled him out, and we made there a grave.
And we put my father near his father, near my grandfather.
By the way, my grandfather's name was also Naftali.
I was named after him.
The next day, next day, all the Jews came.
And we made the proper burial, altogether.
And every Jew was, was, was, was dressed in his tallits and his kittel.
And every Jew we gave a knife in his right hand.
Because there is a Jewish tradition by us, if somebody is killed by somebody, you give to the dead person a knife in his hand.
He should take revenge.
Yes.
This is a local-- it's not a law, but this is how it is.
So everybody--
Yeah.
Everybody from the-- from the people brought a knife, put it in their hand.
And we buried all the people.
And, and, it-- You will read in the book about-- I described there how and what--
These were the leaders of the-- the most prominent people that were shot.
The 20 and two-- and two just for good measure.
And after my father was killed, the whole situation in our house was changed.
Because, after all, he was the patriarch.
And my mother, she started crying.
They took off the crown off her head.
And then-- he was 60 years old.
And then we started to work by the Germans.
And here I was sick.
I came from the hospital.
I got back my old job, instead to go into work every day by heavy work.
So I still started to bring in the merchandise.
And this went-- And every day it was worse, and worse, and worse.
Every day are new orders.
And the orders were terrible.
And I can describe you every order, but it takes too long.
If you read the book, you will see what, every day, happened.
By the way, I mentioned book, so I want to say that I-- my--
--that my wife and I wrote a book.
And I will show you--
I will show it to you.
For the same money, you have a video.
This is one book.
It's called It's written by my wife Amalie Petranker and myself.
And the other book-- this book is about the Holocaust.
The other book is This is my-- from my youth till the war, and it is published by the Syracuse University.
So these people-- if they--
If they will see it, so they will know there is a book.
And now I'm preparing two other books.
So it was, every day, something else.
And we knew that the end is coming.
Because after they killed the 20 Jews, then later, we received bills to pay for the bullets and the gasoline what they used, the gasoline, to come from the other town to make the killing.
And also--
They charged.
And everybody who-- they say, this man was shot seven times, this man five times.
And everybody had to pay for the bullets, and everybody in the community had to pay for their gasoline where they came over.
And there was another time they came.
They wanted to kill, so they made a deal, and they said, for five kilo of gold, they will not kill.
So the community got together five kilo gold.
How they could get the 5 kilo gold, I don't know because we were already in ghetto, and we were starving, and it was already three years' war.
But the money was get-- got together, and the killing was saved.
And in June, in June, the end of June, came the order-- and this was the worst order-- is that the whole town will be taken to Rzeszow, to the larger town.
They gave us three days.
Right away, peasant wagons came, and they loaded the Jews-- the things--
The Jews could take everything what they want.
Everything, they can take.
This was a very small German trick, because they wanted to have every Jewish belongings in one place.
Why should they have, here, the peasants should steal it?
So everything was taken to this large ghetto, which-- Did you family take everything, or did they leave anything?
No.
We, we took we took practically everything.
The only thing we left is furniture.
Furniture [INAUDIBLE].
But everything-- we had a lot of burlap sacks, sacks from our business, so we load.
And we got three wagons.
Usually you got one.
Why?
Because each sister got a wagon.
We loaded it.
And I decided-- my family went the first day.
The first day.
Why?
Because my brother had there somebody in the German police.
He was a commandant in the German police who was not in the Gestapo.
He was a plain policeman, and he used to do business with him.
The German policeman used to go to Vienna.
He was from Vienna.
He used to bring saccharine.
And saccharine was a very important item, because we didn't have sugar.
So he used to bring the saccharine, and he used to buy it from me.
He used to sell it and make profit.
So this German policeman-- he was a meister-- came to my brother.
And he said-- he said he advises him to go with the first day.
Because the first day, the police is in charge.
The second, the third day, maybe the SS men come in.
And he said, when the SS will come in, there will be shootings and all those things.
So he advised him.
So the whole family went the first day.
I only remained alone to go the third day.
And the reason why I remained, because we still couldn't take everything along, and we still had merchandise and a lot of things.
So I had a Polish family, not far from the ghetto.
So I made arrangements that during the night, a whole night, I used to bring [INAUDIBLE] bundles and bundles and bundles to take it out.
And it should be [INAUDIBLE].
And I told her, we will live.
We will take it.
If we wouldn't live, so we want you should have it.
And she took it.
She was very, very accommodating.
And I didn't get back anything, because after I came back for anything, she didn't give it to me.
But this is a different story.
And we-- and my family went to Rzeszow, and they found there by our distant cousin.
In Rzeszow, nobody could find a place.
Because in a place of 30,000 Jews, 100,000 were pushed in from all the towns.
Jews were laying on the street.
Children were looking for their parents.
And on the way-- Also, to Rzeszow was 32 kilometers.
Nobody could ride on the wagon.
Everybody had to walk.
There were old people.
The Germans said this.
Now, the-- sometimes-- the Polish police was also going to watch.
Now, if a German was there and there was a small child or a cripple, he would allow her-- allow the person to go on the wagon.
Not a Polish policeman.
If a Polish policeman was by this wagon, nobody could go on this.
And on the way, they killed people.
And in, in, in Rzeszow it was like a madhouse.
People on the street, not finding--
Rzeszow is R-Z-E-S-Z-O-W.
And they were-- then, later, I came to the Rzeszow the third day.
I brought mostly food, because I got from the people beans and--
I, I came with the other people-- not by myself.
And I brought food, like old, dried food [INAUDIBLE].
And I brought it.
And flour.
I took it, meanwhile, from the peasants.
And I came there, and all my family was in the same place.
In a basement.
Because a distant cousin had, before the war-- he was in the coal business.
So in his yard, he had little coal bins for the coal.
So they gave him over one coal bin, and there was the family.
After two days, when I was in Rzeszow, came out the order that the landkommissar-- it means the head of our town-- his name was Fardonye.
Walter Fardonye I am writing a lot about him.
He was the most vicious person in the world.
He, with his own hand, killed maybe 500 people.
So he said he wants to have 100 young Jewish boys to go back to Kolbuszowa, because he needs them to destroy the ghetto.
Not only Kolbuszowa.
He picked out 100 boys from Glogow, Sokolow, from other towns which belong to our county because Kolbuszowa was the county seat.
And I volunteered, with my brother, to go back within the 100.
And we walked back to our ghetto.
In-- we were-- they established a labor camp in the synagogue, and this was our camp.
And we started to work to destroying the ghetto.
They didn't want to leave anything, so we took a path, house by house, the ghetto where we lived for 300 years.
It took us about three months.
Meanwhile, after two weeks, all Rzeszow was liquidated by actions to Belzec.
They made four akcja.
The first action was 12,000 [?
Jews. ?]
Akcja, we used to call akcja.
It was a day when they said, today is a liquidation.
This was called an 'akcja.' It's 'action.'
So there were four akcjas.
The first akcja, they took 12,000 Jews from Rzeszow. 6,000 were loaded on trains, on cattle cars, and they brought them to Belzec.
6,000 Were taken to a-- to a forest in Glogow, not far from Kolbuszowa, where ditches-- holes-- were dug the day before, and they shot all the people.
And they shot them-- half of them were not killed.
They fell in, and the people on top of it, people on top of it.
And they killed 6,000 in this day.
Later, when we were in the woods, one joined us-- a man who was one of the people who fell in, but he was not killed.
And it took him four days to come out of this hole where he was covered with people.
After four days, naked he came out, and he came to our group in the woods.
Well, this was, in Rzeszow, four actions.
They liquidated--
Oh, we were-- we were-- when I was in Kolbuszowa, destroying the houses.
My-- when my mother was taken away-- this was the 7th of July-- she somehow gave to somebody a letter.
Now, you ask, why did I survive?
Somebody survived.
Somebody survived-- he had a legacy what gave him the strength to survive.
Otherwise, nobody could survive.
Because the only merciful things a German did, it was to kill the person.
Because to live like an animal with these tortures for six years, it was unbelievable.
They used, later, to call it a gnadenschuss.
A gnadenschuss, when one was already near dead, a gnadenschuss, it means a mercy killing.
So if they shot him, they called it a gnadenschuss.
So, so when they brought-- one legacy I have, what gave me strength-- my father's yelling, revenge.
Till today.
The second one, my mother sent a letter to somebody.
What did the letter say?
And the letter said-- and this was her words-- you are my youngest.
You are strong, you are young, you are smart.
If somebody will make it, it will be you.
She always believed, if somebody will make it, it will be you.
Try to survive.
Don't break down because you are an orphan.
[INAUDIBLE] Let your-- being an orphan not break you down.
Try to survive.
And after the war, go to America and tell it to Avrum.
Go to Palestine and tell it to David.
Go to the world, and tell the whole world what the German murderers did to us.
No, I don't have it.
But this is the--
They said-- This-- and this gave me strength.
How many times I said to myself, it's enough.
And this gave me--
And this gave me strength to survive-- those two legacies.
And for this reason, when I was in Poland, I took revenge as much as I could.
Now--
